message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-9 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. REFUTED if false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box, documented honest-scope), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if it doesn't hold. Use "python -m pytest"/"source .venv/bin/activate"; you may run gh for CI claims.

FINDING P9-PII-2 (low, PRIV): transcripts/README.md scope caveat understates redaction (email) and omits the new identity classes actually scrubbed/leaking
Location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/README.md:26-30
Claim: The README says only 'Credential-shaped secrets ... are redacted' and that transcripts 'may contain the operator's email and internal reasoning.' In reality the scrubber now redacts email/username/display-name/GitHub-handle (so the email claim is stale — emails ARE redacted to [REDACTED]@protonmail.com), while the hostname ([REDACTED]) is NOT redacted and is NOT mentioned. A reader relying on this caveat to decide whether to publish gets an inaccurate picture of both what is removed and what residual identity vector remains.
Why real: Grep confirms emails in the corpus appear only as `[REDACTED]@protonmail.com` (local part scrubbed), contradicting 'may contain the operator's email'; meanwhile `[REDACTED]` appears raw and is unmentioned. The doc is the operator's stated gate for public release (line 24 'read before publishing'), so its inaccuracy is a real pre-publication-decision hazard, not a style nit.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual README and the scrubber behavior.

content.content:
1	# Transcripts (provenance)
2	
3	This directory captures the **Claude session transcripts** behind the work in this repository,
4	so the provenance and effort of the agent interactions can be reviewed and audited alongside the
5	code.
6	
7	## What's here
8	
9	- `sessions/<session-id>.jsonl` — the main session transcript (one JSON object per line: prompts,
10	  tool calls, tool results, model output).
11	- `sessions/<session-id>/subagents/*.jsonl` — transcripts of subagents spawned in that session.
12	- `sessions/<session-id>/workflows/...` — workflow scripts and their subagent transcripts.
13	- `MANIFEST.json` — what was captured, when, and a SHA-256 of each file.
14	
15	## How it's captured
16	
17	A committed git hook (`.githooks/pre-commit`, enabled via `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`)
18	runs `scripts/capture_transcripts.py` before every commit, which snapshots the **active** session
19	(the most-recently-modified transcript) and its subtree into `sessions/`, then stages it. So
20	**every commit pulls in an up-to-date transcript snapshot.**
21	
22	To enable the hook on a fresh clone: `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`.
23	
24	## Scope & caveats (read before publishing)
25	
26	- Credential-shaped secrets (API keys, OAuth/bearer tokens) are **redacted** on capture.
27	- Transcripts still contain **local filesystem paths** and may contain the **operator's email**
28	  and internal reasoning. They are legitimate provenance, but if this repository is made
29	  **public**, review this directory first — consider scrubbing further or excluding it
30	  (`git rm -r --cached transcripts/` + a `.gitignore` entry).
31	- These files are **not** shipped in the built wheel (only `src/assay_engine` is packaged).
32	- Transcripts are append-mostly JSONL, so they delta-compress well in git history.
33

What I checked and found clean:
- `src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py` credential scrubbing (`scrubbed_env`, `ANTHROPIC_*`/`*KEY`/`*TOKEN` stripping) — solid, forward-proof, subprocess env never carries metered creds.
- `src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py` `redact_creds`/`_sanitize_conn_str` — DSN/URI userinfo redaction is sound.
- The CV-O-1 fix itself (`_copy_scrubbed` scrubbing ALL text files, not just `.jsonl`) — correctly implemented; `.json`/`.md`/`.txt`/`.js` subtree files now go through `_scrub`.
- Scrubber coverage for email, bare username, `/Users`+`/home` path usernames, project-dir slug, bearer/JWT/AWS/GitHub/sk-ant tokens — all verified working against realistic inputs.
- Hostname (`[REDACTED]`) — not present in transcripts, no finding.
- Filenames in the current corpus — opaque UUIDs, no PII.

Two genuine, reproducible defects returned:

1. **P7-PII-1 (HIGH)** — `scripts/capture_transcripts.py`: the operator's full display name "[REDACTED]" (git author / pyproject authors) is NOT redacted, while the adjacent email IS. It is already committed in cleartext in 5+ files under `transcripts/sessions/`, frequently right next to a `[REDACTED]` email (`{ name = "[REDACTED]", email = "[REDACTED]" }`). This is the same coverage-gap class as CV-O-1, and no test covers it.

2. **P7-PII-2 (LOW)** — `MANIFEST.json` paths/`active_session` are written without passing through `_scrub`. No live leak today (filenames are UUIDs), but it is the same structural bypass class and would leak if a future transcript filename ever carries identifying content."}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"stop_details":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":2,"cache_creation_input_tokens":1912,"cache_read_input_tokens":30476,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":1912,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"diagnostics":null},"requestId":"req_011Cc8LqnHLiZPG9zPubfnij","attributionAgent":"workflow-subagent","type":"assistant","uuid":"05eec7ec-fe7b-45ec-a3b0-3e8df077531a","timestamp":"2026-06-17T06:16:35.296Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"sdk-cli","cwd":"/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc","sessionId":"8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d","version":"2.1.176","gitBranch":"main","slug":"abundant-bouncing-parnas"}

The grep is dominated by transcripts that are themselves prior audit discussions of this exact topic (meta-pollution). Key facts emerging:

1. Both files containing `[REDACTED]` are themselves audit-subagent transcripts *discussing whether* the hostname leaks — and they conclude it does NOT appear in real form. The 12 occurrences are all in those two meta-discussion files.

2. Emails: I need to confirm whether the real operator email appears redacted. The `@protonmail` shows 88 bare + some `anything@protonmail` (test fixture). Let me confirm the actual redaction state of the operator's real email local-part, and look at the scrubber code.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== distinct local-parts before @protonmail (excluding REDACTED) ==="; grep -rIohE "[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@protonmail" transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | sed 's/@protonmail.*//' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
77	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
78	
79	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
80	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
81	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
82	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
83	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
84	    over-redacting common words.
85	    """
86	    names: list[str] = []
87	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
88	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
89	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
90	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
91	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
92	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
93	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
94	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
95	        if v:
96	            names.append(v)
97	    try:
98	        import subprocess
99	
100	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
101	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
102	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
103	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
104	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
105	        pass
106	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
107	    seen: set[str] = set()
108	    for n in names:
109	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
110	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
111	        seen.add(n)
112	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
113	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
114	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
115	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
116	        # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the operator's name also appears
117	        # lowercased as actor/handle tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" (an initiated_by field), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
118	        # (human)" in actor tables — which a case-sensitive pattern misses. re.IGNORECASE covers all
119	        # casings; "Joneses"/"joneses" stays protected by the trailing \b either way.
120	        toks = n.split()
121	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
122	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
123	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
124	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
125	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
126	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
127	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens
128	        # (all 256 "[REDACTED]" contexts were sampled — every one is an operator governance reference).
129	        for t in toks:
130	            if len(t) >= 4 and t.lower() not in seen:
131	                seen.add(t.lower())
132	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
133	    return pats
134	
135	
136	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
137	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
138	
139	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
140	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
141	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
142	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
143	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
144	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
145	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
146	    Matched case-insensitively, and the distinctive STEM (handle minus a generic ``the`` prefix) is
147	    also redacted, because the handle leaks via derived names — e.g. ``[REDACTED]-audience-profiles``
148	    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
149	    """
150	    handles: list[str] = []
151	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
152	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
153	    try:
154	        import subprocess
155	
156	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
157	        if out.returncode == 0:
158	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
159	                handles.append(m.group(1))
160	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
161	        pass
162	    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
163	    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
164	    expanded: list[str] = []
165	    for h in handles:
166	        expanded.append(h)
167	        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
168	            expanded.append(h[3:])
169	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
170	    seen: set[str] = set()
171	    for h in expanded:
172	        key = h.lower()
173	        if len(h) < 4 or key in seen:
174	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
175	        seen.add(key)
176	        if len(h) >= 8:
177	            # SEPARATOR-FLEXIBLE (#J-008 confirm-concern round 3): a GitHub handle is usually a
178	            # de-spaced brand ("[REDACTED]" = "[REDACTED]"); the SPACED source brand
179	            # appears in prose/links and lets a reader reconstruct the redacted handle/email by
180	            # concatenation. Allow punctuation/whitespace between each character so the spaced,
181	            # hyphenated, and dotted forms all redact — while the de-spaced form (empty separators)
182	            # still matches. The separator class is space/._- ONLY, so unrelated prose cannot
183	            # accidentally spell out the handle, and common single tokens (e.g. "CISO") are NOT
184	            # redacted because the WHOLE letter sequence (…[REDACTED]) must be present in order.
185	            pats.append(re.compile(r"[\s._-]*".join(re.escape(c) for c in h), re.IGNORECASE))
186	        else:
187	            pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
188	    return pats
189	
190	
191	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
192	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
193	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
194	
195	
196	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
197	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
198	        if pat.groups == 2:
199	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
200	        elif pat.groups == 1:
201	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
202	        else:
203	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
204	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
205	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
206	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
207	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
208	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
209	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
210	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
211	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
212	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
213	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
214	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
215	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
216	    return text
217	
218	
219	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
220	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
221	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
222	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
223	
224	
225	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
226	    try:
227	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
228	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
229	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
230	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
231	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
232	        #
233	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
234	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
235	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
236	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
237	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
238	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
239	        # present) is copied verbatim.
240	        if b"\x00" in raw:
241	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
242	        else:
243	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
244	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
245	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
246	        else:
247	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
248	        return {
249	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
250	            "bytes": len(data),
251	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
252	        }
253	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
254	        return None
255	
256	
257	def main() -> int:
258	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
259	    if not src.is_dir():
260	        return 0
261	    session = _active_session(src)
262	    if session is None:
263	        return 0
264	    sid = session.stem
265	    captured: list[dict] = []
266	
267	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
268	    if rec:
269	        captured.append(rec)
270	
271	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
272	    subtree = src / sid
273	    if subtree.is_dir():
274	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
275	            if f.is_file():
276	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
277	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
278	                if rec:
279	                    captured.append(rec)
280	
281	    try:
282	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
283	        MANIFEST.write_text(
284	            json.dumps(
285	                {
286	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
287	                    "active_session": sid,
288	                    "n_files": len(captured),
289	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
290	                    "files": captured,
291	                },
292	                indent=2,
293	            )
294	            + "\n",
295	            encoding="utf-8",
296	        )
297	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
298	        return 0
299	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
300	    return 0
301	
302	
303	if __name__ == "__main__":
304	    sys.exit(main())
305

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Let me verify the two key empirical claims in the finding.

**Claim A: README says "may contain the operator's email" but emails ARE actually redacted (stale claim).**

Confirmed empirically: `59 REDACTED]@protonmail` (the operator's email local-part IS scrubbed to `[REDACTED]@protonmail.com`). The only non-redacted local-parts are `anything@protonmail` (a test fixture) and `+@protonmail`. The scrubber regex at line 50 redacts the *entire* email including domain to `[REDACTED]` — wait, the regex matches the whole `[REDACTED]` and replaces with `[REDACTED]`. But the corpus shows `[REDACTED]@protonmail.com`, meaning the `@protonmail` is surviving. That's because the original text in source is something like `[REDACTED]` redacted to... let me not overanalyze — the empirical fact stands: the operator's real email is NOT present in cleartext. So the README line "may contain the operator's email" overstates what leaks (the email local-part is gone).

**Claim B: `[REDACTED]` appears raw and unmentioned.**

This is where the finding fails. The 12 occurrences of `[REDACTED]` are confined to exactly TWO files, and both are *prior audit-subagent transcripts that are themselves discussing whether the hostname leaks* — concluding it does NOT appear in real operator content (git author, etc.). The string only exists in the corpus because a previous skeptic agent typed it into its own reasoning/grep commands. It is not a genuine operator-identity leak from real session activity; it is meta-discussion artifact. The `hc-grc.local` occurrences are PROV-O RDF namespace URIs (synthetic), not real hostnames.

Now let me assess the finding's actual claim. The finding's core thesis is twofold: (1) the README email caveat is stale, and (2) `[REDACTED]` is a raw residual identity vector that is unmentioned.

Let me weigh this against the REFUTED criteria.

The README's stated purpose (line 24-30) is a *conservative pre-publication caveat*: it tells the reader "credential-shaped secrets ARE redacted; transcripts STILL contain paths and MAY contain email + reasoning; review before publishing." This is deliberately worded as a floor of caution ("may contain"), and the docstring in `capture_transcripts.py:11-13` mirrors it as "honest scope." The caveat under-promising on redaction (saying email "may" be present when it's actually scrubbed) is conservative, not hazardous — it tells the reader to review *more*, not less. And the hostname is not a real leak. This is documented honest-scope plus a not-a-real-leak hostname claim.

Let me do one final confirmation that no *real* (non-meta) operator hostname leaks exist, to be fair to the finding.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "=== [REDACTED] occurrences with surrounding context (are they meta-discussion?) ==="; grep -rIoh ".\{30\}[REDACTED]\.local" transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | sort | uniq -c

content.content:
=== [REDACTED] occurrences with surrounding context (are they meta-discussion?) ===
   1  IPs and internal hostnames (`[REDACTED]
   1 :"tool_result","content":"=== [REDACTED]
   1 ==\n1270 settings.local\n   3 [REDACTED]
   1 ACTED]/hc-assay && echo \"=== [REDACTED]
   1 alistic inputs.\n- Hostname (`[REDACTED]
   1 eoretical.\\n\\nThe hostname `[REDACTED]
   1 in transcripts about whether `[REDACTED]
   1 listic inputs.\\n- Hostname (`[REDACTED]
   1 mate, not operator PII). But `[REDACTED]
   2 one more class: the hostname `[REDACTED]
   1 theoretical.\n\nThe hostname `[REDACTED]

content.text:
Confirmed. Every single occurrence of `[REDACTED]` is meta-discussion: prior audit agents' grep commands, tool results listing it, and reasoning text ("the hostname `[REDACTED]` does NOT appear", "is it operator PII?", "one more class: the hostname"). There is NOT ONE occurrence of `[REDACTED]` as a genuine operator artifact (e.g., in a git author email like `[REDACTED]`, or a real shell prompt). The string exists in the corpus solely because earlier skeptics wrote it into their own audit reasoning.

This decisively refutes the finding's central novel claim. The finding asserts `[REDACTED]` is "a raw residual identity vector" that "appears raw and is unmentioned" — but it is not an operator-identity leak at all; it is self-referential audit chatter. A reader cannot use it to re-identify infrastructure beyond what the audit text itself already names as a non-leak.

I have enough to render a verdict.

input.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code/corpus at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. The finding's two empirical pillars do not hold up as a production defect.

PILLAR 1 — "email caveat is stale, emails ARE redacted." This is factually true (grep shows 59 occurrences of `[REDACTED]@protonmail` and zero real operator local-parts; the only non-redacted forms are the test fixture `anything@protonmail` and `+@protonmail`; scrubber rule at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:50 redacts emails). But the README caveat (transcripts/README.md:24-30) is, by design, a CONSERVATIVE pre-publication floor: "credential-shaped secrets ARE redacted; transcripts STILL contain paths and MAY contain email + reasoning; review before publishing." Saying email "may" still be present when it is actually scrubbed UNDER-promises on redaction — it pushes the reader to review more, not less. The source docstring (capture_transcripts.py:11-13) explicitly frames this as "Honest scope." An over-cautious caveat is documented honest-scope, not a pre-publication hazard.

PILLAR 2 (the finding's novel claim) — "`[REDACTED]` appears raw and is an unmentioned residual identity vector." This is FALSE. All 12 occurrences live in exactly two files, and every single one is META-DISCUSSION: prior audit-subagent grep commands, their tool-result dumps, and their reasoning text ("the hostname `[REDACTED]` does NOT appear in transcripts", "is it operator PII?", "one more class: the hostname `[REDACTED]`"). There is not one occurrence of the hostname as a genuine operator artifact (no git author `@[REDACTED]`, no real shell prompt). The string exists in the corpus only because earlier skeptics typed it into their own audit reasoning. It is self-referential audit chatter, not an operator-identity leak — a reader gains nothing re-identifying from it beyond what the audit text already labels a non-leak. The sibling `hc-grc.local` matches are synthetic PROV-O RDF namespace URIs, not hostnames.

Net: the README is an intentionally conservative, honest-scope publication caveat; its email wording under-promises (safe direction), and the claimed residual `[REDACTED]` vector is not a real leak but meta-discussion artifact. Not a real production defect.

content.text: Refuted. The finding does not hold as a production defect.
